extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: {'type': 'Lab-scale gantry crane', 'main_components': [{'component': 'Physical crane', 'description': 'A small-scale mechanical gantry crane system, likely used for educational or research purposes.'}, {'component': 'Controller', 'description': 'Embedded or software-based system responsible for controlling the crane's movements and operations.'}], 'operational_domain': {'environment': 'Lab-scale (controlled indoor environment)', 'purpose': 'Research, education, and validation of digital twin methodologies', 'key_operations': ['Lifting and transporting objects', 'Kinematic movements (e.g., horizontal/vertical displacement)']}, 'key_characteristics': [{'characteristic': 'Modularity', 'description': 'Designed to be easily integrated with digital twin frameworks for bidirectional data exchange.'}, {'characteristic': 'Reproducibility', 'description': 'Publicly available and relies on open-source/freely available tools, facilitating reproducibility in research.'}, {'characteristic': 'Scalability', 'description': 'Serves as a foundational use case for validating digital twin concepts, potentially adaptable to larger systems.'}, {'characteristic': 'Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Integration', 'description': 'Explicitly designed as a cyber-physical system, enabling seamless interaction between physical and digital components.'}]}
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: {'services': [{'service_type': 'Optimal Control', 'description': 'Provides algorithms or methods for optimizing the crane's control parameters (e.g., trajectory planning, energy efficiency, or load balancing).', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Engineers', 'Control System Developers'], 'capabilities': ['Real-time adjustment of control parameters', 'Optimization of crane movements for efficiency']}, {'service_type': 'Historical Data Logging', 'description': 'Collects and stores operational data from the physical crane for analysis and validation.', 'target_users': ['Data Analysts', 'Researchers', 'Maintenance Teams'], 'capabilities': ['Long-term data storage', 'Time-series data collection for trend analysis']}, {'service_type': 'Data Visualization', 'description': 'Visualizes real-time and historical data to provide insights into crane performance and system health.', 'target_users': ['Operators', 'Researchers', 'Educators'], 'capabilities': ['Real-time monitoring dashboards', 'Visualization of kinematic models and operational states']}, {'service_type': 'Continuous Validation', 'description': 'Validates the digital twin's accuracy by continuously comparing digital and physical system states.', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Validation Engineers'], 'capabilities': ['Real-time synchronization checks', 'Fault detection and discrepancy resolution']}, {'service_type': 'Fault Detection', 'description': 'Monitors the system for anomalies or faults, alerting users to potential issues.', 'target_users': ['Maintenance Teams', 'Operators'], 'capabilities': ['Anomaly detection in operational data', 'Predictive maintenance alerts']}]}
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: {'software_platforms': [{'tool': 'CAD Models', 'role': 'Represents the physical structure of the crane in a digital format (e.g., 3D models for visualization and simulation).', 'technologies': ['Not specified (likely standard CAD tools like SolidWorks, Fusion 360, or Blender)']}, {'tool': 'Kinematic Model', 'role': 'Mathematical representation of the crane's movement mechanics, enabling simulation and control.', 'technologies': ['Not specified (likely MATLAB/Simulink, Python with SymPy, or custom implementations)']}], 'development_tools': [{'tool': 'Open-Source Software', 'role': 'Ensures reproducibility and accessibility of the digital twin exemplar.', 'technologies': ['Freely available and commonly used software (e.g., Python, ROS, or other open-source frameworks)']}], 'simulation_engines': [{'tool': 'Digital Twin Framework', 'role': 'Enables bidirectional data flow between physical and digital systems, supporting services like control and validation.', 'technologies': ['Not specified (likely custom-built or leveraging existing frameworks like NVIDIA Omniverse, Siemens MindSphere, or PTC ThingWorx)']}], 'databases': [{'tool': 'Historical Data Storage', 'role': 'Stores operational and sensor data for logging and analysis.', 'technologies': ['Not specified (likely SQL/NoSQL databases or time-series databases like InfluxDB)']}], 'specialized_technologies': [{'technology': 'DarTwin Notation', 'role': 'A notation system for describing the evolution of digital twin systems, used in this exemplar for system documentation.', 'description': 'Facilitates clear communication of system changes and updates in the digital twin lifecycle.'}, {'technology': 'Continuous Validation Methods', 'role': 'Methods for ensuring the digital twin remains synchronized with the physical system.', 'description': 'Includes real-time and offline validation techniques to detect discrepancies.'}]}
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) primarily adopt **decentralized or ad-hoc architectures** rather than centralized frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **virtual machines** with **prerecorded data**, implying a **static or batch-oriented synchronization** approach for behavioral adaptation, though no explicit synchronization frequency or latency requirements are specified. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages a **Raspberry Pi with sensors** for real-time data acquisition, suggesting **near-real-time synchronization** for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction applications. The **gantry crane system** (logistics/cyber-physical domain) follows a **C4 Model architecture**, which typically supports **modular, service-oriented decomposition**, but lacks explicit details on temporal granularity or synchronization mechanisms. No hierarchical multiplicities (e.g., parent-child Digital Twin instances) are described; coordination is implied via **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., Docker, VMs) and **message brokers** (e.g., ActiveMQ in GreenhouseDT) for inter-component communication, though no formal orchestration protocol (e.g., pub/sub frequencies) is documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation in the provided exemplars follows an **ad-hoc, lightweight orchestration pattern** without reliance on commercial frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) to manage **behavioral adaptation** between the physical greenhouse and its Digital Twin, with **virtual machines** hosting pre-recorded data for offline analysis. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar employs **AutomationML** for Digital Twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, indicating a **cloud-centric, service-oriented constellation** with **three distinct applications** (runtime visualization, simulation, prediction) coordinated via Azure’s ecosystem. The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) adopts a **C4 Model architecture**, emphasizing **context and container diagrams** for software-hardware integration but lacks explicit details on system-wide coordination mechanisms. **Containerization/virtualization** (e.g., Docker, VMs) is a recurring theme for **modular deployment**, while **single-board computers (Raspberry Pi)** serve as edge nodes for sensor-data acquisition. No formal orchestration framework (e.g., Kubernetes, service mesh) is mentioned; coordination relies on **ad-hoc integration** of components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems exhibit **limited but domain-specific horizontal integration** with external systems. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates with **InfluxDB** (for time-series data storage) and **ActiveMQ** (for message brokering), but no external interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or enterprise system dependencies are documented. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for Digital Twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** (e.g., Azure IoT, Logic Apps) for pipeline orchestration, implying **cloud-based integration** with potential enterprise systems via Azure’s native protocols (e.g., REST APIs, event grids). The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) lacks explicit details on external integration but aligns with **cyber-physical system (CPS) architectures**, which typically rely on **industrial protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, PROFINET) for factory/warehouse integration. No cross-Digital Twin or enterprise system interoperability (e.g., federated twins, shared ontologies) is described; integration patterns are **domain-isolated** and **ad-hoc**, with no standardized protocols for data exchange between twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical system (e.g., greenhouse, air quality management) and its digital counterpart, enabling dynamic adjustments in real-time. In the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar, the digital twin leverages **prerecorded data** embedded in virtual machine images for simulation and validation, while relying on an **InfluxDB** time-series database for structured data storage and an **ActiveMQ** message broker for event-driven communication between components. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes **AutomationML-based digital twin models** for indoor air quality simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization, with a focus on **model-driven engineering** principles. The digital twin pipeline integrates **Microsoft Azure tools** for cloud-based execution, enabling scalable model deployment and data processing. Model relationships are implicit in the ad-hoc architecture, where physical system data (e.g., sensor inputs) is ingested into the digital twin for behavioral replication and predictive analytics. No explicit geometric or analytical models are detailed, but the use of **virtualization (VMs)** and **containerization** suggests modular, reusable model components tailored to specific use cases (e.g., greenhouse control, air quality monitoring).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time environmental monitoring, including parameters critical to indoor air quality (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, humidity, temperature). The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly detail sensor specifications but implies integration with physical systems via **prerecorded data** in virtual machines, suggesting sensor inputs are pre-processed or simulated. No explicit details on **sampling frequencies**, **spatial distribution**, or **data transmission rates** are provided, though the use of **InfluxDB** and **ActiveMQ** in GreenhouseDT implies structured, time-series data collection with potential for high-frequency updates. The **AutomationML-based digital twin** in the Air Quality exemplar suggests standardized sensor data formats for interoperability, but no precision/accuracy specifications are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral fidelity**, where both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin dynamically adjust to maintain synchronization. Validation is implied through **prerecorded data** in virtual machines, enabling offline testing of model accuracy against historical system behavior. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **runtime validation** via three applications: **visualization** (real-time monitoring), **simulation** (scenario testing), and **prediction** (forecasting virus spread risks). Uncertainty quantification is not explicitly addressed, but the use of **Microsoft Azure tools** suggests cloud-based analytics for error detection (e.g., anomaly detection in sensor data). No formal **verification methods** (e.g., model checking, calibration protocols) or **quality assurance processes** are detailed, though the **ad-hoc architecture** implies iterative refinement based on empirical feedback. The **AutomationML** standard in the Air Quality exemplar may inherently support fidelity through structured model exchange, but no quantitative metrics (e.g., RMSE, confidence intervals) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves real-time data transmission from the **Physical Crane** to the **Digital Twin** via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program). The data types transmitted include **crane state measurements** (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the gantry and puck, controller feedback, and environmental factors like load weight). The communication protocol is not explicitly specified, but the **Logging Application** continuously streams this data to a **TimescaleDB database** for storage and analysis. The transmission frequency is not quantified, but the system implies **high-frequency updates** to ensure accurate trajectory validation. **Preprocessing steps** are not detailed, but the **Continuous Validation Service** suggests raw data is compared against simulated trajectories. **Data quality assurance** is implicit via the **Continuous Validation Service**, which calculates distance metrics between executed and simulated trajectories and flags deviations exceeding an acceptable threshold. **Data integration mechanisms** include the **MQTT Broker**, which facilitates signaling between services, ensuring synchronized data flows for validation and visualization purposes.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this system is mediated by the **Control Application**, which generates and transmits control commands to the **Physical Crane** via the **Physical Crane Interface**. The **command types** include **optimal movement trajectories** (generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service**) with no residual swinging motion at the end. The **control message format** is not explicitly defined, but the system implies structured commands aligned with the crane’s kinematics model. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly described, though the **Continuous Validation Service** ensures executed trajectories match simulations, indirectly validating command execution. **Feedback systems** are present via the **Logging Application**, which captures real-time crane state data post-command execution. **Conflict resolution** is not detailed, but the **Dashboard** notifies operators if thresholds are breached, allowing manual intervention. **Command execution confirmation** is implied through the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares executed trajectories against simulations and triggers alerts if discrepancies exceed thresholds.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** relies on a **service-oriented architecture** with the following key elements: **Communication protocols** include **MQTT** for inter-service signaling (via the **MQTT Broker**) and unspecified protocols for data transmission between the **Physical Crane Interface** and **Logging Application**. The **network topology** is not explicitly detailed, but the system implies a **centralized architecture** with the **Database (TimescaleDB)** as the core data repository. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified, but the **Continuous Validation Service** suggests low-latency communication to enable real-time trajectory comparison. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not described, though the **MQTT Broker** and **Database** imply fault-tolerant design. **Security protocols** are not mentioned, but the system’s reliance on **TimescaleDB** and **Grafana Dashboard** suggests standard database and web security measures (e.g., authentication, encryption) are assumed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **Digital Twin’s Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in terms of cloud/edge/on-premise, but the system’s components suggest a **hybrid or on-premise deployment model**. The **Database (TimescaleDB)** and **MQTT Broker** imply **local hosting** for low-latency operations, while the **Dashboard (Grafana)** and **Simulation/Trajectory Services** may leverage **edge computing** for real-time processing. **Computational requirements** are not quantified, but the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** require sufficient CPU/GPU resources for kinematics modeling and trajectory optimization. **Storage requirements** are met by **TimescaleDB**, which handles time-series data efficiently. **Scalability mechanisms** are not described, though the modular design (e.g., separate services) allows for potential horizontal scaling. **Availability/reliability assurance** is implied by the **Continuous Validation Service** and **Dashboard alerts**, but no explicit redundancy or failover strategies are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management) employ an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar leverages **virtual machines (VMs)** with pre-recorded data for ease of installation, utilizing **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering to facilitate real-time communication between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates a **Raspberry Pi** with embedded sensors for real-time data acquisition and an **AutomationML-based digital twin** for model-driven engineering. Both exemplars utilize **containerization or virtualization technologies** (e.g., VMs) to ensure reproducibility and dissemination of the codebase. **Version control** is implicitly managed via GitHub repositories ([GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)), though explicit versioning strategies (e.g., semantic versioning, branching models) are not detailed. The **evolution management** of the digital twins appears to focus on **behavioral adaptation** (e.g., self-adaptive mechanisms in GreenhouseDT) and **runtime capabilities** (e.g., simulation, prediction, and visualization in Air Quality Management), with no explicit mention of formalized change management or compliance-driven lifecycle transitions. The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar (logistics/cyber-physical domain) similarly adopts an ad-hoc approach, leveraging **C4 Model** for architectural visualization but lacks explicit lifecycle governance details.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **three core decision-support applications**: (1) **runtime visualization** of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., sensor data from the Raspberry Pi), (2) **simulation** of air quality dynamics under varying conditions (e.g., occupancy, ventilation), and (3) **prediction** of virus spread risks using the digital twin’s AutomationML-based model. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **self-adaptive behavioral insights**, enabling real-time adjustments to both the physical greenhouse (e.g., climate control) and its digital twin via **InfluxDB-stored data** and **ActiveMQ-mediated feedback loops**. **Analytics capabilities** are implied through the use of **Microsoft Azure tools** in the Air Quality Management pipeline, though specific algorithms (e.g., ML models, statistical methods) are not detailed. **User interaction** is facilitated via runtime interfaces (e.g., dashboards for visualization), while **confidence measures** for recommendations are not explicitly quantified. The **gantry crane** exemplar lacks documented insights or decision-making capabilities in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adheres to **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, a standardized format for engineering data exchange, ensuring interoperability with other industrial systems. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly mention compliance with industry standards but relies on **commonly used technologies** (InfluxDB, ActiveMQ) that are widely adopted in IoT and cyber-physical systems. Neither exemplar references **certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO 22400 for digital twins, IEC 62443 for cybersecurity). The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar does not provide details on standardization, though its architecture is visualized using the **C4 Model**, a software-centric notation. **Interoperability standards** are only explicitly mentioned for AutomationML in the Air Quality Management case.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
